sentences.base_language: My name is Sofía. I live in a small apartment near the city centre.
sentences.target_language: Me llamo Sofía. Vivo en un pequeño apartamento cerca del centro de la ciudad.
sentences.grammarNote: 'Me llamo' (literally 'I call myself') is the standard way to say 'my name is'. It uses the reflexive verb llamarse. 'Del' = de + el, a mandatory contraction in Spanish.

sentences.base_language: Every morning I wake up, eat bread with butter and drink coffee.
sentences.target_language: Todas las mañanas me despierto, como pan con mantequilla y bebo café.
sentences.grammarNote: 'Me despierto' uses the reflexive verb despertarse (to wake up). Many daily routine verbs are reflexive: levantarse (to get up), vestirse (to get dressed), acostarse (to go to bed).

sentences.base_language: I am happy — or at least, I feel calm.
sentences.target_language: Estoy feliz — o al menos, me siento tranquila.
sentences.grammarNote: Both 'estoy feliz' and 'me siento tranquila' use estar, not ser — they describe temporary emotional states. 'Sentirse' is reflexive: me siento, te sientes, se siente.

sentences.base_language: Today is a good day. I need to go to the market near my neighbourhood.
sentences.target_language: Hoy es un buen día. Necesito ir al mercado cerca de mi barrio.
sentences.grammarNote: 'Al' = a + el, a mandatory contraction. 'Buen' is the shortened form of 'bueno' used before masculine singular nouns: buen día, buen amigo, buen hombre.

sentences.base_language: I put on my coat and walk straight ahead, then turn left at the corner.
sentences.target_language: Me pongo el abrigo y camino todo recto, luego giro a la izquierda en la esquina.
sentences.grammarNote: 'Me pongo' uses the reflexive ponerse (to put on clothing): me pongo el abrigo, te pones los zapatos. 'Todo recto' = straight ahead. 'Luego' = then / afterwards.

sentences.base_language: The street is busy but not too noisy. The sun is hot on my face and shoulder.
sentences.target_language: La calle está concurrida pero no es demasiado ruidosa. El sol calienta mi cara y mi hombro.

sentences.base_language: At the market, an old man named Paulo is a chef. He is also a musician — on weekends he plays his guitar in the park.
sentences.target_language: En el mercado, un hombre mayor llamado Paulo es cocinero. También es músico — los fines de semana toca su guitarra en el parque.
sentences.grammarNote: 'Los fines de semana' = on weekends. The definite article marks recurring time: los lunes (on Mondays), los veranos (in summers). 'Tocar' = to play an instrument; 'jugar' = to play a game or sport.

sentences.base_language: Today he is cooking soup with onion, tomato, and fresh herbs. It smells delicious.
sentences.target_language: Hoy está preparando sopa con cebolla, tomate y hierbas frescas. Huele delicioso.
sentences.grammarNote: 'Está preparando' = estar + gerund, for actions happening right now. Compare: 'cocina sopa' (he cooks soup, habitually) vs 'está cocinando sopa' (he is cooking soup, right now).

sentences.base_language: "Good morning!" he says. "Would you like to taste this?" "Yes, please," I say. The soup is hot, a little spicy, and very tasty.
sentences.target_language: —Buenos días —dice—. ¿Te gustaría probar esto? —Sí, por favor —digo. La sopa está caliente, un poco picante y muy sabrosa.
sentences.grammarNote: Spanish literature uses dashes (—) for dialogue instead of quotation marks. '¿Te gustaría...?' = Would you like...? (conditional, polite offer). 'Probar' = to taste / to try.

sentences.base_language: I buy a big bowl and also some fruit — an apple, a banana, and an orange. I put them in my bag.
sentences.target_language: Compro un tazón grande y también fruta — una manzana, un plátano y una naranja. Los meto en mi bolsa.

sentences.base_language: Near the market there is a small café. I enter and sit at a table by the window.
sentences.target_language: Cerca del mercado hay un pequeño café. Entro y me siento en una mesa junto a la ventana.
sentences.grammarNote: 'Hay' (from haber) = there is / there are. It's invariable: 'hay un café', 'hay tres cafés'. Never use 'es' or 'está' for existence. 'Junto a' = next to / beside.

sentences.base_language: A young waiter comes over. His name is Marcos. He is a student at the university and works here part-time.
sentences.target_language: Un joven camarero se acerca a mí. Se llama Marcos. Es estudiante en la universidad y trabaja aquí a tiempo parcial.

sentences.base_language: "What do you recommend?" I ask. He thinks for a moment and smiles. "The cheese sandwich is very good today. And the orange juice."
sentences.target_language: —¿Qué recomiendas? —pregunto. Piensa un momento y sonríe. —El sándwich de queso está muy bueno hoy. Y el zumo de naranja.
sentences.grammarNote: 'Recomendar' is a stem-changing verb (e→ie): recomiendo, recomiendas, recomienda. The stem change happens in all forms except nosotros and vosotros.

sentences.base_language: While I wait, I look out the window. A police officer is crossing the street. Two children are running in the park.
sentences.target_language: Mientras espero, miro por la ventana. Un policía cruza la calle. Dos niños corren en el parque.

sentences.base_language: I feel something warm — not just the coffee in my hands, but something deeper. Joy, maybe. Or comfort.
sentences.target_language: Siento algo cálido — no solo el café entre mis manos, sino algo más profundo. Alegría, tal vez. O comodidad.
sentences.grammarNote: 'No solo... sino...' = not only... but... Used when the second clause contrasts with or replaces the first. Compare: 'no solo... también...' (not only... also...), which adds rather than replaces.

sentences.base_language: After my meal, I leave the café and walk to the library. I love the library — it is quiet and full of good things.
sentences.target_language: Después de comer, salgo del café y camino hasta la biblioteca. Me encanta la biblioteca — está tranquila y llena de buenas cosas.
sentences.grammarNote: 'Me encanta' = I love (literally 'it enchants me'). Like gustar, encantar agrees with what is loved: me encanta el libro (I love the book), me encantan los libros (I love the books).

sentences.base_language: I am learning a new language — it is not always easy, and sometimes I feel stress, but I am calm today.
sentences.target_language: Estoy aprendiendo un nuevo idioma — no siempre es fácil, y a veces siento estrés, pero hoy estoy tranquila.
sentences.grammarNote: 'Estoy aprendiendo' = I am (in the process of) learning. The present progressive (estar + gerund) emphasises ongoing activity. 'A veces' = sometimes. 'Hoy estoy tranquila' — note estoy, not soy: temporary state.

sentences.base_language: On my way home, I pass the hospital on the corner. My friend Amara is a nurse there — hardworking, sometimes exhausted, but her work gives her a sense of purpose.
sentences.target_language: De camino a casa, paso por el hospital de la esquina. Mi amiga Amara es enfermera allí — muy trabajadora, a veces agotada, pero su trabajo le da un sentido de propósito.
sentences.grammarNote: 'De camino a' = on the way to. 'Paso por' = I pass by (pasar por). 'Le da' = gives her — the indirect object pronoun 'le' refers to Amara. Note: 'es enfermera' without the article (professions omit it after ser).

sentences.base_language: I arrive home just before dinner. I cook rice with chicken, a little salt, pepper, and oil.
sentences.target_language: Llego a casa justo antes de cenar. Cocino arroz con pollo, un poco de sal, pimienta y aceite.

sentences.base_language: I eat alone, but I do not feel lonely. I think about Paulo and his soup, Marcos and his smile, Amara and her courage.
sentences.target_language: Como sola, pero no me siento sola. Pienso en Paulo y su sopa, en Marcos y su sonrisa, en Amara y su valentía.
sentences.grammarNote: 'Pensar en' = to think about (a person or thing). Different from 'pensar que' (to think that / to believe): 'pienso en ti' (I think about you) vs 'pienso que tienes razón' (I think you're right).

sentences.base_language: I feel grateful. Here, in my small apartment, in this busy city, I am home.
sentences.target_language: Me siento agradecida. Aquí, en mi pequeño apartamento, en esta ciudad bulliciosa, estoy en casa.
